retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of functional asplenism is contingent upon the prevalence and incidence rates of the precipitating disease. Below are several epidemiological studies on functional asplenia:

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Complications -- Thrombocytosis
retrieved_docs.content: Venous thrombosis in patients with asplenism is associated with platelet counts greater than 600 to 800 k/µL and occurs in approximately 5% of patients. Thrombocytosis can also cause arterial thrombosis, leading to myocardial infarction and cerebrovascular accidents. Some patients take aspirin or even a cytoreductive agent such as hydroxyurea or anagrelide to reduce the risk of thrombotic events.

retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Canavan disease is an autosomal recessive disorder associated with a gene locus on chromosome 17. It is caused by a mutation in the ASPA gene that encodes the enzyme aspartoacylase. This mutation results in an aspartoacylase deficiency and the accumulation of N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA) in the brain. NAA is believed to cause oligodendrocyte dysfunction, consequential spongiform changes, and degeneration of myelin in the phospholipid layer of axons. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of functional asplenia in thalassemia can vary depending on the type and severity of the disease, as well as the patient's age. Results from a 2013 study published in the Journal of Pediatric Hematology/Oncology revealed that functional asplenia was present in approximately 50% to 75% of patients diagnosed with beta-thalassemia major, the most severe form of the disease. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Allgrove Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Allgrove’s syndrome results from mutations of the AAAS gene located on chromosome 12q13 that encodes a nuclear envelope protein known as ALADIN (alacrimia-achalasia-adrenal insufficiency neurologic) and highly expresses in different human tissues. [5] [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: When recognized and managed adequately with vaccines and proper prophylaxis, people with functional asplenism can lead a long, relatively healthy life despite being immunocompromised. Mortality varies widely in patients who are functionally asplenic depending on the infecting pathogen, underlying comorbidities, and access to vaccines and prophylactic antibiotics, in addition to other factors. Recognition of early sepsis with appropriate treatment can substantially reduce overall mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Functional asplenism is characterized by the absence of phagocytic activity while maintaining the presence of splenic tissue, and occurs as a result of:

retrieved_docs.title: Acrokeratoelastoidosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In familial cases that constitute the majority of the reports to date implicate a genetic abnormality involving chromosome 2. A hypothesis of altered expression of a gene-protein complex akin to p34 involvement in PPKP1 has been previously mentioned (vide supra ). In sporadic cases, factors like excessive sun-exposure and chronic trauma may serve as possible triggering factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Asplenia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal (GI) disorders such as Celiac disease, Whipple disease, and inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) can be associated with hypofunction of the spleen. [10] Infectious disease states and acquired immune deficiency syndromes, including HIV. [10] Hepatic disorders such as alcoholic liver disease, cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and hepatorenal syndrome. [10] Rheumatologic conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) have been associated with reduced spleen function. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy to Prevent Myocardial Infarction and Death
retrieved_docs.content: Controversy remains regarding the use of aspirin for primary prevention in patients at variable risk of coronary CAD. Major recent trials have shown no significant reduction in cardiovascular events, often with an increased risk of bleeding. The ARRIVE trial identified patients at moderate risk who received aspirin for primary prevention, and demonstrated no reduction in the primary outcomes of CV death, MI, unstable angina, stroke or transient ischemic attack. Furthermore, there was an increase in gastrointestinal bleeding (HR = 2.11, CI 95%, 1.36 - 3.28). Similar findings were found in the ASCEND trial, which studied diabetic patients, and the ASPREE trial which followed more than 19,000 patients at least 65 years of age, who were free from CVD or dementia. These findings may suggest that aspirin should not be routinely prescribed for the sole purpose of primary prevention, and that a careful risk-benefit assessment - as pertains to cardiovascular outcomes versus bleeding - needs to be performed before initiation of life-time therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Knowledge of the spleen's crucial and protective functions shows the importance of recognizing functional asplenism, a condition most commonly seen in patients with autoimmune diseases and various hematological disorders. OPSI, as well as thrombotic events, are serious medical emergencies that require prompt intervention. Prompt recognition of the illness's high-risk nature may limit morbidity.The simple interventions of immunizations and prophylactic antibiotics are expected to reduce the frequency of OPSI and decrease its mortality despite the controversy over antibiotic use in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Further research exploring and understanding the underlying mechanisms and intricate processes leading to functional asplenism is essential to develop more effective treatments and targeted therapies to improve the overall management of patients affected by these complex hematologic conditions and enhance the quality of life for those experiencing functional asplenism.

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are four genes which have been implicated as the molecular basis of Fahr disease i.e., loss of function mutation in the gene SLC20A2 encoding type 3 sodium-dependent phosphate transporter 2 (PiT2) on chromosome 8p (40%), a mutation in gene XPR1 that encodes for a retroviral receptor with phosphate export function on chromosome 1q (2%), a mutation in the gene which encodes the receptor for members of the platelet-derived growth factor family- gene PDGFRB on chromosome 5q (2%), and gene PDGFB on chromosome 22q (11%). 46% of cases have some unknown gene mutations. Other loci that have been linked to Fahr disease include IBGC1 locus at chromosome 14q, a locus at chromosome 2q, and another one at chromosome 8. [3] Since, some genes have been implicated in Fahr disease, the term idiopathic basal ganglia calcification, used previously, should be avoided.

retrieved_docs.title: Asplenia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with asplenia can be asymptomatic, while some can present with malaise, fatigue, fever, or encephalopathy. On physical examination, a normal spleen is not palpable.  When enlarged, it can typically be palpated below the costophrenic border on deep inspiration. In patients with various degrees of functional asplenia (autosplenectomy), the spleen will usually not be palpable due to chronic atrophy. Patients may also present with signs or symptoms indicative of underlying infection. Severe infection due to Streptococcus pneumoniae , Haemophilus influenzae , and Neisseria meningitides are the most common bacterial infections recognized in these patients. As such, it is important to assess for nuchal rigidity in these patients if they present with fever. In particular, pneumococcal meningitis can be up to four times more fatal in asplenic patients compared to other forms of bacterial meningitis and over 6 times more likely to result in poor health outcomes. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics: Patients with functional asplenia may be prescribed long-term antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent infections. Antibiotics such as penicillin or amoxicillin are typically used for this purpose, but local microorganism resistance should be considered. In addition, antimalarial prophylaxis should be given to patients traveling to malaria-endemic areas. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Complications -- Acute infectious purpura fulminans
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infectious purpura fulminans is a rapidly progressive syndrome of hemorrhagic skin necrosis associated with acute infection due to encapsulated organisms (usually Streptococcus pneumoniae or group A streptococci) resulting in disseminated intravascular coagulation with a strong predilection for patients with asplenism or functional hyposplenism. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 395
retrieved_docs.content: disorder (اضطراب مخي وعائي), reflecting that these sequelae constitute a subset of broader cerebrovascular disorders. Collectively, these hierarchical and cross‑disciplinary relationships organize diagnoses by linking specific entities such as congenital vascular dilatation, late cerebrovascular sequelae, and rectal pathology to their larger disease families, underscoring the importance of integrated surgical, pathophysiological, pathological and virological perspectives in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Ampicilin and ranitidin are frequently noted to co‑occur in clinical settings, often being prescribed together; ampicilin is used as a broad‑spectrum antibiotic for bacterial infections, while ranitidin is employed to reduce gastric acid secretion, suggesting that patients receiving ampicilin may also be given ranitidin to mitigate gastrointestinal side effects or to address concurrent acid‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 606
retrieved_docs.content: of these three agents suggests they are often used together in clinical scenarios such as cardiovascular disease management where gastric protection (ranitidine) and anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) are required alongside cardiac glycoside therapy (digoxin). Recognizing these co‑occurrence trends can aid clinicians in anticipating drug interactions and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin and ranitidin are frequently noted to co‑occur, indicating that these two agents are often used together or observed in the same clinical contexts. This co‑occurrence suggests that ranitidin may be administered alongside aspirin, potentially to mitigate gastrointestinal side effects associated with aspirin therapy, reflecting an integrated approach to patient care when both medications are indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2824
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics is recognized as an allowed qualifier for gout and other crystal arthropathies, for intestinal infection, and for فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperuricemia). This indicates that hereditary factors may influence susceptibility to these conditions, underscoring the relevance of genetic considerations in their diagnosis, risk assessment, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: chronic inflammatory small bowel disease and in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, underscoring the importance of recognizing a chronic clinical course across diverse organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2087
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease and late effects of cerebrovascular disease are both classified under the broader category اضطراب مخي وعائي (cerebrovascular disorder). Within this hierarchy, other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease is specifically linked to تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ (fibrous sclerosis), indicating that some poorly defined cerebrovascular conditions may involve fibrotic changes. Recognizing that these subtypes fall under اضطراب مخي وعائي and that other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease can be associated with fibrous sclerosis helps clinicians organize cerebrovascular diagnoses and anticipate potential fibrotic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: a clinical correlation between constipation and diarrhea reflects alternating bowel‑motility disturbances that may accompany these cardiac pathologies, underscoring the need for integrated, multidisciplinary patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1426
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin and digoxin are frequently noted to co‑occur in clinical practice, indicating that these two medications are often prescribed together or observed concurrently in patients, particularly in the management of cardiovascular conditions where antiplatelet therapy with aspirin may be combined with the cardiac glycoside effects of digoxin. This co‑occurrence highlights the importance of monitoring for potential drug interactions and ensuring coordinated care when both agents are part of a patient’s treatment regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.
